title: Insert Interval
code: static int[][] Insert(int[][] intervals, int[] newInterval)
// Phase 2: overlapping intervals are merged into newInterval.

frames.caption: Insert newInterval = [4,8] into a pre-sorted, disjoint list. Start the single O(n) pass at i = 0.
frames.note: No sort is needed: the input is already sorted by start and non-overlapping. That is exactly what turns this into a linear three-phase scan — copy the left region, merge the overlapping middle into newInterval, then copy the right region. We carry one index i across all three phases.

frames.caption: Phase 1: intervals[0] = [1,2]. end 2 < newInterval.start 4 → copy as-is.
frames.note: Phase 1 copies intervals that finish strictly before the new one begins (interval.end < newInterval.start). Because the list is sorted by start AND disjoint, anything ending to the left of new.start sits entirely to its left and can never overlap it — so we pass it through untouched. This is the cheap left region; the moment an interval reaches up to new.start we stop and switch to merging.

frames.caption: Copied [1,2] to result. Advance to i = 1.
frames.note: Phase 1 copies intervals that finish strictly before the new one begins (interval.end < newInterval.start). Because the list is sorted by start AND disjoint, anything ending to the left of new.start sits entirely to its left and can never overlap it — so we pass it through untouched. This is the cheap left region; the moment an interval reaches up to new.start we stop and switch to merging.

frames.caption: Phase 1 stops: intervals[1] = [3,5] has end 5 ≥ newInterval.start 4. Switch to merging.
frames.note: Phase 1 copies intervals that finish strictly before the new one begins (interval.end < newInterval.start). Because the list is sorted by start AND disjoint, anything ending to the left of new.start sits entirely to its left and can never overlap it — so we pass it through untouched. This is the cheap left region; the moment an interval reaches up to new.start we stop and switch to merging.

frames.caption: Phase 2: intervals[1] = [3,5]. start 3 ≤ newInterval.end 8 → overlap, merge it in.
frames.note: Phase 2 absorbs every interval that overlaps the new one. The test is interval.start <= newInterval.end (touching at a point counts, e.g. 8 <= 8). We widen newInterval on BOTH sides — start = Math.Min(...), end = Math.Max(...) — never blindly take the current interval, because a short interval fully inside new must not shrink it. The new interval keeps growing as it swallows neighbours, and is appended exactly once after the run ends.

frames.caption: Widen: newInterval = [min([4,8].s, 3), max([4,8].e, 5)] = [3,8].
frames.note: Phase 2 absorbs every interval that overlaps the new one. The test is interval.start <= newInterval.end (touching at a point counts, e.g. 8 <= 8). We widen newInterval on BOTH sides — start = Math.Min(...), end = Math.Max(...) — never blindly take the current interval, because a short interval fully inside new must not shrink it. The new interval keeps growing as it swallows neighbours, and is appended exactly once after the run ends.

frames.caption: Phase 2: intervals[2] = [6,7]. start 6 ≤ newInterval.end 8 → overlap, merge it in.
frames.note: Phase 2 absorbs every interval that overlaps the new one. The test is interval.start <= newInterval.end (touching at a point counts, e.g. 8 <= 8). We widen newInterval on BOTH sides — start = Math.Min(...), end = Math.Max(...) — never blindly take the current interval, because a short interval fully inside new must not shrink it. The new interval keeps growing as it swallows neighbours, and is appended exactly once after the run ends.

frames.caption: Widen: newInterval = [min([3,8].s, 6), max([3,8].e, 7)] = [3,8].
frames.note: Phase 2 absorbs every interval that overlaps the new one. The test is interval.start <= newInterval.end (touching at a point counts, e.g. 8 <= 8). We widen newInterval on BOTH sides — start = Math.Min(...), end = Math.Max(...) — never blindly take the current interval, because a short interval fully inside new must not shrink it. The new interval keeps growing as it swallows neighbours, and is appended exactly once after the run ends.

frames.caption: Phase 2: intervals[3] = [8,10]. start 8 ≤ newInterval.end 8 → overlap, merge it in.
frames.note: Phase 2 absorbs every interval that overlaps the new one. The test is interval.start <= newInterval.end (touching at a point counts, e.g. 8 <= 8). We widen newInterval on BOTH sides — start = Math.Min(...), end = Math.Max(...) — never blindly take the current interval, because a short interval fully inside new must not shrink it. The new interval keeps growing as it swallows neighbours, and is appended exactly once after the run ends.

frames.caption: Widen: newInterval = [min([3,8].s, 8), max([3,8].e, 10)] = [3,10].
frames.note: Phase 2 absorbs every interval that overlaps the new one. The test is interval.start <= newInterval.end (touching at a point counts, e.g. 8 <= 8). We widen newInterval on BOTH sides — start = Math.Min(...), end = Math.Max(...) — never blindly take the current interval, because a short interval fully inside new must not shrink it. The new interval keeps growing as it swallows neighbours, and is appended exactly once after the run ends.

frames.caption: Merge stops: intervals[4].start 12 > newInterval.end 10. Append the widened newInterval [3,10] once.
frames.note: The first interval whose start exceeds newInterval.end ends the merge run. Nothing from here on can overlap new — starts only increase, so the gap is permanent. That means newInterval is final: we append the single widened interval now, then everything left is pure copy work.

frames.caption: Pushed newInterval [3,10] to result — it absorbed every overlapping interval.
frames.note: The first interval whose start exceeds newInterval.end ends the merge run. Nothing from here on can overlap new — starts only increase, so the gap is permanent. That means newInterval is final: we append the single widened interval now, then everything left is pure copy work.

frames.caption: Phase 3: copy remaining intervals[4] = [12,16] (starts after newInterval ends).
frames.note: Phase 3 copies the untouched right region: every remaining interval starts strictly after new ends, so each is disjoint from new and from the ones already placed. No comparison is needed — just drain the rest. The whole pass is O(n) precisely because the pre-sorted, non-overlapping input let us walk the three contiguous regions (left / absorbed / right) in one sweep with no sort.

frames.caption: i = 5 = n — loop ends. Result: [1,2] [3,10] [12,16].
frames.note: The single pass is done: three contiguous regions handled with one index and no sort. newInterval [4,8] swallowed [3,5], [6,7], and [8,10] (note 8 ≤ 8 touches) to emerge as [3,10], leaving [1,2] on the left and [12,16] on the right. Total cost O(n) time, O(n) output space — the linear win that the pre-sorted, disjoint input buys you.
